html:
V

The Gift

Mrs.¬ÝArb was listening to a customer and giving change.

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòAnd when you‚Äôve got children of your own,‚Äô she said, ‚Äòand when you‚Äôve got children of your own,‚Äô that was her remark,‚Äù the customer, an insecurely fat woman, was saying.

‚ÄúJust so,‚Äù Mrs.¬ÝArb agreed, handing the change and pushing a little parcel across the counter. She ignored Mr.¬ÝEarlforward completely. He stood near the door, while the fat customer repeated once more what some third person had remarked upon a certain occasion. The customer‚Äôs accent was noticeably vulgar in contrast with Mrs.¬ÝArb‚Äôs. Mrs.¬ÝArb was indeed very ‚Äúwell spoken.‚Äù And she contrasted not merely with the customer but with the shop.

There were dozens of such little shops in and near King‚Äôs Cross Road. The stock, and also the ornamentation, of the shop came chiefly from the wholesalers of advertised goods made up into universally recognizable packets. Several kinds of tea in large quantities, and picturesque, bright tea-signs all around the shop. Several kinds of chocolate, in several kinds of fancy polished-wood glazed stands. (But the chocolate of one maker was in the stand of another.) All manner of patent foods, liquid and solid, each guaranteed to give strength. Two competitors in margarine. Scores of paper bags of flour. Some loaves; two hams, cut into. A milk-churn in the middle of the shop. Tinned fruits. Tinned fish. Tinned meats. And in the linoleum-lined window the cakes and bonbons which entitled the shop to style itself ‚Äúconfectioner‚Äôs.‚Äù Dirty ceiling; uneven dark wood floor; frowsy, mysterious corners; a shabby counter covered with linoleum in black-and-white check, like the bottom of the window. One chair; one small, round, iron table. No cash-desk. No writing apparatus of any sort. A smell of bread, ham and biscuits. A poor little shop, showing no individuality, no enterprise, no imagination, no potentiality of reasonable profits. A shop which saved the shopkeeper from the trouble of thinking for himself. The inevitable result of big advertising, and kept up to the average mark by the constant visitation of hurried commercial travellers and collectors who had the magic to extract money out of empty tills.

And Mrs.¬ÝArb, thin, bright, cheerful, with scintillating eyes; in a neat check dress and a fairly clean white apron! Yes, she was bright, she was cheerful, she had a keen face. Perhaps that was what had attracted Mr.¬ÝEarlforward, who was used to neither cheerfulness nor brightness. Yet he thought: ‚ÄúIt would have been just about the same if she‚Äôd been a gloomy woman.‚Äù Perhaps he had been attracted because she had life, energy, downrightness, masterfulness.

‚ÄúGood evening, Mr.¬ÝEarlforward. And what can I do for you?‚Äù She greeted him suddenly, vivaciously, as the fat customer departed.

She knew him, then! She knew his real name. She knew that his name did not accord with the sign over his shop. Her welcoming smile inspired him, as alcohol would have inspired him had he ever tasted it. He was uplifted to a higher plane of existence. And also, secretly, he was a little bit flurried; but his demeanour did not betray this. A clock struck rapidly in some room behind the shop, and at the sound Mrs.¬ÝArb sprang from behind the counter, shut and locked the shop door, and drew down its blind for a sign to the world that business was over for the day. She had a fine movement with her. In getting out of her way Mr.¬ÝEarlforward strove to conceal his limp as much as possible.

‚ÄúI thought I‚Äôd just look in about that cookery-book you wanted,‚Äù said he.

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs very kind of you I‚Äôm sure,‚Äù said she. ‚ÄúBut I really don‚Äôt think I shall need it.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh!‚Äù

‚ÄúNo! I think I shall get rid of this business. There‚Äôs no doing anything with it.‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm sorry to hear that,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝEarlforward. And he was.

‚ÄúIt isn‚Äôt as if I didn‚Äôt enjoy it‚ÅÝ‚Äîat first. Quite a pleasant change for me to take something in hand. My husband died two years ago and left me nicely off, and I‚Äôve been withering up ever since, till this came along. It‚Äôs no life, being a widow at my age. But I couldn‚Äôt stand this either, for long. There‚Äôs no bounce to this business, if you understand what I mean. It‚Äôs like hitting a cushion.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou‚Äôve soon decided.‚Äù

‚ÄúI haven‚Äôt decided. But I‚Äôm thinking about it.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ You see, it‚Äôs a queer neighbourhood.‚Äù

‚ÄúQueer?‚Äù He was shocked, perhaps a little hurt, but his calm tone disclosed nothing of that. He had a desire to explain to Mrs.¬ÝArb at great length that the neighbourhood was one of almost unique interest.

‚ÄúWell, you know what I mean. You see, I come from Fulham‚ÅÝ‚ÄîChelsea you might call it. I‚Äôm not saying that when I lived in this shop before‚ÅÝ‚Äîeighteen years ago, is it?‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI‚Äôm not saying I thought it was a queer neighbourhood then. I didn‚Äôt‚ÅÝ‚Äîand I was here for over a year, too. But I do now.‚Äù

‚ÄúI must confess it hasn‚Äôt struck me as queer.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou know this King‚Äôs Cross Road?‚Äù Mrs.¬ÝArb proceeded with increased ardour. ‚ÄúYou know it? You‚Äôve walked all along it?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes.‚Äù

‚ÄúSo have I. Oh! I‚Äôve looked about me. Is there a single theatre in it? Is there one music-hall? Is there one dance-hall? Is there one picture theatre? Is there one nice little restaurant? Or a teashop where a nice person could go if she‚Äôd a mind?‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ And yet it‚Äôs a very important street; it‚Äôs full of people all day. And you can walk for miles round here and see nothing. And the dirt and untidiness! Well, I thought Fulham was dirty. Now look at this Riceyman Square place, up behind those funny steps! I walked through there. And I lay there isn‚Äôt one house in it‚ÅÝ‚Äînot one‚ÅÝ‚Äîwithout a broken window! The fact is, the people about here don‚Äôt want things nice and kept.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I‚Äôm not meaning you‚ÅÝ‚Äîcertainly not! But people in general. And they don‚Äôt want anything fresh, either. They only want all the nasty old things they‚Äôve always had, same as pigs. And yet I must say I admire pigs, in a way. Oh, dear!‚Äù She laughed, as if at herself, a tinkling laugh, and looked down, with her steady agreeable hand still on the door.

Twice before she had looked down. It was more than coyness, better than coyness, more genuinely exciting. When she laughed her face crinkled up very pleasantly. She had energy. All the time her body made little movements. Her glance varied, scintillating, darkling. Her tone ceaselessly varied. And she had authority. She was a masterful woman, but masterful in a broad-minded, genial manner. She was experienced, and had learnt from experience. She must be over forty.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ And still, somehow girlish! Best of all, she was original; she had a point of view. She could see. Mr.¬ÝEarlforward hated Clerkenwell to be damned. Yet he liked her to damn it.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ And how natural she was, dignified, but not ceremonious, willing to be friends at once! He repeated to himself that from the first sight of her he had known her to be a highly remarkable creature.

‚ÄúI brought the book along,‚Äù he said, prudently avoiding argument. She took it amiably from him, and out of politeness inspected it again.

‚ÄúYou shall have it for ninepence. And you might be needing it after all, you know.‚Äù

With her face still bent towards Snacks and Titbits she raised her eyes to his eyes‚ÅÝ‚Äîit seemed roguishly.

‚ÄúI might! I might!‚Äù She shut the book with a smart snap. ‚ÄúBut I won‚Äôt go beyond sixpence, thank you all the same. And not as I don‚Äôt think it‚Äôs very kind of you to bring it over.‚Äù

What a woman! What a woman! She was rapidly becoming the most brilliant, attractive, competent, and comfortable woman on earth; and Mr.¬ÝEarlforward was rapidly becoming a hero, a knight, a madman capable of sublime deeds. He felt an heroical impulse such as he had never felt. He fought it, and was beaten.

‚ÄúSee here,‚Äù he said quietly, and with unconscious grandeur. ‚ÄúWe‚Äôre neighbours. I‚Äôll make you a present of the book.‚Äù

Did she say, as a silly little creature would have said: ‚ÄúOh, no! I couldn‚Äôt possibly. I really couldn‚Äôt‚Äù? Not a bit. She said simply:

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs most kind of you, Mr.¬ÝEarlforward. It really is. Of course I accept it with pleasure. Thank you.‚Äù

And she looked down, like a girl who has received a necklace and clasped it on her neck. Yes, she looked down. The moment was marvellous to Mr.¬ÝEarlforward.

‚ÄúBut I do think you‚Äôre a little hard on Riceyman Square,‚Äù he said, as she unlocked the door for his departure.

She replied gaily and firmly: ‚ÄúNot one house without a broken pane!‚Äù She insisted and held out her hand.

‚ÄúWell, we must see one day,‚Äù said he.

She nodded.

‚ÄúAnd if there is,‚Äù she said, ‚ÄúI shall pay you a shilling for the book. That‚Äôs fair.‚Äù

She shook hands. Mr.¬ÝEarlforward crossed the space between her shop and his with perfect calmness, and as he approached his door he took from his pocket with the mechanical movement of regular habit a shining key.
